findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether `handle_create`'s `comment_errors` path (apply_handlers.py lines 117-118) also needs the flag-gated `outcome["error"]` assignment — if so, add it to the 'What' edit targets explicitly.
evidence: Plan 'What': 'apply_handlers.py: when comment_errors is present AND _rebar_env("RECONCILER_FAIL_SILENT_NOOP")=="1", set outcome["error"]' — does not distinguish handle_create from handle_update
apply_handlers.py lines 117-118: `if comment_errors: outcome["comment_errors"] = list(comment_errors)` inside handle_create — no flag-gate proposed or present
apply_handlers.py line 231: flag gate exists only inside handle_update's silent-noop block
findings.finding: `handle_create` in `apply_handlers.py` also collects `comment_errors` (lines 117-118) and sets them on `outcome`, but the plan's 'What' section describes applying the flag-gate only to `apply_handlers.py` in general — the existing silent-noop canary pattern (flag check at line 231) lives only in `handle_update`. With the proposed change, CREATE-path comment failures remain non-fatal even when the flag is ON.
findings.location: What section; apply_handlers.py handle_create lines 117-118 vs handle_update lines 195-196 and 231
scenarios: A CREATE mutation's comment sub-op fails; flag is ON; outcome has comment_errors but no error key → reconcile.py:1052 does not count it as a mutation_failure → exit 0 despite the flag.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a parallel flag check after line 118 in handle_create: `if comment_errors and _rebar_env('RECONCILER_FAIL_SILENT_NOOP', '0') == '1': outcome['error'] = f'comment-errors: {comment_errors}'`. Document this in the plan's What section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm (and explicitly specify in the plan) that the flag guard applies to **both** `handle_create` (lines 117-118) and `handle_update` (lines 195-196), not only the update path that mirrors the existing canary.
evidence: Plan text: 'apply_handlers.py: when comment_errors is present AND _rebar_env("RECONCILER_FAIL_SILENT_NOOP")=="1", set outcome["error"] ... mirroring the existing total-no-op canary flag check'
The 'existing total-no-op canary flag check' is ONLY in handle_update (line 231); handle_create (lines 117-118) has no canary analogue today
handle_create at line 117-118 populates comment_errors non-fatally with no flag guard in the current code
AC-1: 'an outcome carrying comment_errors counts toward mutation_failures' — no carve-out for CREATE-path outcomes
findings.finding: The plan's 'What' section specifies applying the flag guard 'mirroring the existing total-no-op canary flag check' and cites apply_handlers.py lines 117-118 and 195-196. Lines 195-196 are handle_update's comment_errors block (adjacent to the canary). Lines 117-118 are handle_CREATE's comment_errors block — but handle_create has no canary today and the plan does not explicitly state that the flag guard must also be added there. If handle_create's comment_errors go unguarded, a CREATE-path comment failure would still not count toward mutation_failures even with the flag ON, violating AC-1's 'an outcome carrying comment_errors counts toward mutation_failures' contract.
scenarios: A CREATE mutation succeeds but its add_comment call fails; the flag is ON; the outcome has comment_errors but no error key → mutation_failures not incremented → pass exits 0 → silent failure not surfaced despite flag being activated
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence to the 'What' section: 'Apply the same flag guard to handle_create's comment_errors block (lines 117-118) — the CREATE path also accumulates comment_errors non-fatally and must be covered for AC-1 to hold end-to-end.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly state in 'What' whether the flag-gated `comment_errors → outcome['error']` promotion applies to BOTH `handle_create` and `handle_update`, or only to updates, and add an AC unit test scenario for the create path if both are intended.
evidence: apply_handlers.py:99,117-118: handle_create collects comment_errors and sets outcome['comment_errors'] with no flag check
apply_handlers.py:231: canary flag check exists only in handle_update
Plan 'What': 'when comment_errors is present AND _rebar_env("RECONCILER_FAIL_SILENT_NOOP")=="1", set outcome["error"]' — does not say which handler(s)
findings.finding: The plan says to add comment_errors→outcome['error'] promotion 'mirroring the existing total-no-op canary flag check', but `handle_create` (apply_handlers.py:92-119) also collects `comment_errors` (lines 99, 117-118) and has no canary check. The plan's 'What' section does not specify whether `handle_create`'s comment_errors path also receives the flag-gated promotion, leaving the handler scope of the new decision rule undefined.
scenarios: Executor adds the promotion only in handle_update (following the 'mirroring' language), leaving handle_create's comment_errors silent even with the flag ON — AC1's unit test only exercises update if the test fixture uses an update mutation.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit deliverable to the 'What' section for extending the silent-no-op canary to cover comments and labels (not just links), matching the scope of AC item 3.
evidence: What section describes only: (a) `comment_errors` gating behind the flag, (b) optional telemetry summary line. No mention of extending the existing silent-no-op canary to comments/labels.
AC 3: 'The silent-no-op canary already covers TOTAL no-ops for links/comments/labels (verify + a test for comments/labels, not just links — the (b) audit noted links-only coverage)'
The (b) audit finding of links-only coverage implies code changes/tests are needed, but the What section is silent on this.
findings.finding: AC item 3 requires verifying and extending the silent-no-op canary to cover comments/labels (not just links), but the 'What' section describes no deliverable for this work — it only describes the `comment_errors` gating change and an optional telemetry line. AC item 3 is an orphan criterion with no corresponding described work.
scenarios: Implementer reads What, implements only the comment_errors gating, considers the ticket done, and AC item 3 fails review — blocking merge.
The canary gap (comments/labels not covered) remains open because no one is assigned to fix it per the plan body.
findings.suggested_fix: In the What section, add a bullet: 'Extend the existing total-no-op canary check (`apply_handlers.py`) to cover `comment` and `label` sub-op types, not just `link` (currently links-only per the (b) audit); add unit tests for each.'

evidence: vague/subjective terms present: clean

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the 'verify' placeholder in AC3 with a stated assumption about current coverage and a concrete proving command: either (a) state 'canary currently covers links only; extend it to cover comments/labels and prove with `pytest tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/apply/test_silent_noop_canary.py -k comment_label`' or (b) state 'canary already covers all three; prove with the existing passing test name'.
evidence: Plan text: 'verify + a test for comments/labels, not just links — the (b) audit noted links-only coverage'
The word 'verify' is explicitly listed as a placeholder signal in the E2 rubric checklist ('verify whether' defers a real decision to the executor)
No proving command is given for the verification half of this AC — only the test half is implied
If the canary already covers comments/labels, the AC is trivially satisfied by an existing test; if not, a new canary extension is required — but the plan does not commit to either outcome
findings.finding: AC3 uses the word 'verify' to defer an investigation of current canary coverage (links vs. comments/labels) to the executor, making it a placeholder rather than a decision. The plan does not state what the current state IS (links-only? or already covers all three?), what the executor should do if coverage is already complete, and provides no proving command for the verification step — only 'verify + a test'.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3: 'The silent-no-op canary already covers TOTAL no-ops for links/comments/labels (verify + a test for comments/labels, not just links…)'
scenarios: Executor investigates and finds coverage already exists → writes a test that passes trivially and declares done, with no change to canary logic
Executor investigates and finds a gap → must extend canary logic without a specified design, effectively doing design work outside the plan
Reviewer cannot tell which scenario the plan intends, blocking sign-off
findings.suggested_fix: Replace AC3 text with: 'The silent-no-op canary is extended to cover comments and labels in addition to links. Proving command: `pytest tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/apply/test_silent_noop_canary.py -k "comment or label"` — this test must be NEW (did not exist before this change) and GREEN after it.' If the canary already covers all three, state that fact explicitly and point to the existing test name.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the env var name to a single canonical spelling (`RECONCILER_FAIL_SILENT_NOOP` or `REBAR_RECONCILER_FAIL_SILENT_NOOP`) used consistently across Why, What, Sequencing, and all AC items.
evidence: Why: 'only hard-fails behind `RECONCILER_FAIL_SILENT_NOOP`'
What: '_rebar_env("RECONCILER_FAIL_SILENT_NOOP")=="1"'
Sequencing: 'ACTIVATE (set `REBAR_RECONCILER_FAIL_SILENT_NOOP=1` in `.github/workflows/reconcile-bridge.yml`)'
AC 5: 'Flag activated in reconcile-bridge.yml AFTER the Jira cleanup'
findings.finding: The environment variable name is inconsistent across sections: 'Why' and 'What' use `RECONCILER_FAIL_SILENT_NOOP`, while 'Sequencing' and AC item 5 use `REBAR_RECONCILER_FAIL_SILENT_NOOP`. An implementer reading these sections would set two different env vars.
findings.location: Why section (~line 218 reference), What section (`_rebar_env("RECONCILER_FAIL_SILENT_NOOP")`), Sequencing section (`REBAR_RECONCILER_FAIL_SILENT_NOOP=1`), AC item 5
scenarios: Implementer sets `RECONCILER_FAIL_SILENT_NOOP=1` in the workflow (as written in What), but the Sequencing section and AC point to `REBAR_RECONCILER_FAIL_SILENT_NOOP=1` — the flag never fires, or fires from the wrong var.
Code written against one name, workflow configured with the other, leaving the gating logic permanently dead.
findings.suggested_fix: Standardize on `REBAR_RECONCILER_FAIL_SILENT_NOOP` (the `REBAR_` prefix matches the workflow convention implied by the Sequencing section) and update every mention in Why and What accordingly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Harmonize the env var name in the 'Why' and 'What' sections to use `REBAR_RECONCILER_FAIL_SILENT_NOOP` (the full env var name) consistently with AC5 and Sequencing — remove the bare `RECONCILER_FAIL_SILENT_NOOP` references that omit the `REBAR_` prefix.
evidence: Plan 'Why': 'only hard-fails behind `RECONCILER_FAIL_SILENT_NOOP` (default "0" = warn-only)'
Plan 'What': 'when `comment_errors` is present AND `_rebar_env("RECONCILER_FAIL_SILENT_NOOP")=="1"'
apply_handlers.py:48-55: `_rebar_env` does `os.environ.get(f'REBAR_{name}', default)` — prepends REBAR_
apply_handlers.py:231: `if _rebar_env('RECONCILER_FAIL_SILENT_NOOP', '0') == '1':` — actual env var is REBAR_RECONCILER_FAIL_SILENT_NOOP
test_silent_noop_canary.py:173: `monkeypatch.setenv('REBAR_RECONCILER_FAIL_SILENT_NOOP', '1')` — tests use full name
AC5 and Sequencing: correctly use `REBAR_RECONCILER_FAIL_SILENT_NOOP=1`
findings.finding: The plan's 'Why' and 'What' sections refer to the flag as `RECONCILER_FAIL_SILENT_NOOP` (the bare `_rebar_env` parameter), but the actual environment variable that operators must set is `REBAR_RECONCILER_FAIL_SILENT_NOOP` (because `_rebar_env` prepends `REBAR_` at apply_handlers.py:55). AC5 and Sequencing correctly use the full form, but the 'What' section's prose (`_rebar_env("RECONCILER_FAIL_SILENT_NOOP")=="1"`) is internally consistent at code level only — an operator reading the 'What' section in isolation to configure the new `comment_errors` gate could set the wrong env var name and find the gate silently inert.
scenarios: An operator following the 'What' description to verify the new comment_errors gate sets `RECONCILER_FAIL_SILENT_NOOP=1` in reconcile-bridge.yml instead of `REBAR_RECONCILER_FAIL_SILENT_NOOP=1`; the gate never activates and partial comment failures remain invisible.
A future reviewer checking the plan's premises concludes the flag name in 'Why'/'What' and in AC5/Sequencing refer to different env vars, creating confusion about which is canonical.
findings.suggested_fix: In 'Why': replace '`RECONCILER_FAIL_SILENT_NOOP`' with '`REBAR_RECONCILER_FAIL_SILENT_NOOP`'. In 'What': replace '`_rebar_env("RECONCILER_FAIL_SILENT_NOOP")=="1"'` with '`REBAR_RECONCILER_FAIL_SILENT_NOOP=="1"` (via `_rebar_env("RECONCILER_FAIL_SILENT_NOOP")`)' to clarify the env var name operators use vs. the code-level parameter.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate whether `apply_outbound.py`'s `comment_errors` path (lines 151-156) feeds `reconcile.py`'s `mutation_failures` counter, and if so add it as an edit target in the plan's 'What' section.
evidence: apply_outbound.py lines 151-156: `comment_errors: list[str] = []; update_result = update_one(batch, client, comment_errors=comment_errors); if comment_errors: payload['comment_errors'] = comment_errors`
Plan 'What' section names only `apply_handlers.py` as the edit target for comment_errors gating
ABSENCE: plan does not mention apply_outbound.py or the typed-dispatch comment_errors path
findings.finding: `apply_outbound.py` (lines 151-156) contains a third `comment_errors` collection path that feeds `ApplyResult.payload['comment_errors']` via the typed-dispatch route. This file is not named as an edit target, and it is unclear whether its payload flows into the `outcomes` list counted by `reconcile.py:1052` or is handled separately — leaving an unenumerated consumer of the same failure pattern.
scenarios: Typed-dispatch path executes an outbound update with a failed comment sub-op; flag is ON; apply_outbound.py records comment_errors in ApplyResult.payload but never sets an error; if this payload reaches mutation_failures counting, the failure is invisible.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify whether the `applied_count`-shape manifest path also needs a `failed_count` field written (e.g. in `manifest_renderer.py` / `apply_planning.py`) so that `comment_errors`-promoted failures are counted in `mutation_failures` across ALL reconciler run modes, not only the flat-mutations-list path.
evidence: reconcile.py:1046-1048: `if 'applied_count' in manifest_data: ... mutation_failures = int(manifest_data.get('failed_count', 0))`
manifest_renderer.py: search confirms `failed_count` never written — only `applied_count` and `deferred_count`
apply_planning.py:175: only forwards `applied_count` from rendered, not `failed_count`
Plan 'What': 'set outcome["error"] (so partial comment failures count)' — assumes flat-mutations tally path only
findings.finding: The plan's counter-increment mechanism (setting `outcome['error']` → counts in `mutation_failures`) only works for the flat `mutations`-list tally branch in reconcile.py:1052. The alternate `applied_count`/`failed_count` branch (reconcile.py:1046-1048) reads `manifest_data.get('failed_count', 0)` — a key that manifest_renderer.py and apply_planning.py never write, so it always defaults to 0. The plan does not specify whether `failed_count` must also be produced/incremented in the manifest for this code path.
findings.location: Plan section 'What' / AC item 1; reconcile.py:1046-1052; manifest_renderer.py
scenarios: When the reconciler runs in bootstrap-strict or throttle mode (manifest has `applied_count` shape), comment_errors-promoted errors set outcome['error'] but the manifest never gains `failed_count`, so mutation_failures = 0 and the pass exits 0 — the exact silent failure the plan aims to fix.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in 'What' that `RECONCILER_FAIL_SILENT_NOOP` is the `_rebar_env` argument (which auto-prepends `REBAR_`), making the full OS-level env var `REBAR_RECONCILER_FAIL_SILENT_NOOP`, and verify the new comment_errors branch uses the same `_rebar_env("RECONCILER_FAIL_SILENT_NOOP", "0") == "1"` call form.
evidence: 'Why': `RECONCILER_FAIL_SILENT_NOOP` (default '0' = warn-only)
'What': `_rebar_env("RECONCILER_FAIL_SILENT_NOOP")=="1"`
'Sequencing': `REBAR_RECONCILER_FAIL_SILENT_NOOP=1` in reconcile-bridge.yml
apply_handlers.py:48-55: `_rebar_env` prepends `REBAR_` — never stated in plan
apply_handlers.py:231: `_rebar_env("RECONCILER_FAIL_SILENT_NOOP", "0")` → reads `REBAR_RECONCILER_FAIL_SILENT_NOOP`
findings.finding: The plan's 'Why' and 'What' sections name the flag `RECONCILER_FAIL_SILENT_NOOP` (the bare _rebar_env argument), while 'Sequencing' and AC5 name it `REBAR_RECONCILER_FAIL_SILENT_NOOP` (the actual OS env var). The `_rebar_env` shim convention (prepends `REBAR_`) is never explained in the plan, so an executor could write `os.environ.get('RECONCILER_FAIL_SILENT_NOOP')` directly — a check that would silently never fire because the real env var has the prefix.
scenarios: Executor writes direct os.environ.get('RECONCILER_FAIL_SILENT_NOOP') in the new comment_errors branch — flag never activates, AC1 silently passes tests that set the wrong env var name.
findings.suggested_fix: In 'What', write: "use `_rebar_env(\"RECONCILER_FAIL_SILENT_NOOP\", \"0\") == \"1\"` (same call site as the existing canary at apply_handlers.py:231; the shim expands to the OS var `REBAR_RECONCILER_FAIL_SILENT_NOOP`)."

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either add an AC item that specifies what the telemetry line must contain and when it appears, or explicitly remove the telemetry line from the 'What' section as out-of-scope for this ticket.
evidence: What: 'Optional: a per-pass telemetry summary line (silent-no-op count + comment-error count) — always-on, non-fatal (pure visibility)'
Absence: no AC item tests for or verifies the presence/correctness of this telemetry output
findings.finding: The optional telemetry summary line (per-pass silent-no-op count + comment-error count, always-on, non-fatal) is described as a deliverable in the 'What' section but has no covering acceptance criterion.
findings.location: What section ('Optional: a per-pass telemetry summary line'), Acceptance Criteria (no matching item)
scenarios: Implementer skips the telemetry line; no AC catches its absence. Or implementer implements it incorrectly; no AC validates behavior.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Promote the per-pass telemetry summary (comment-error count + silent-no-op count) from 'Optional' to required, or add an AC line that guarantees the counts appear in structured log output when the flag is ON — so operators can distinguish comment_errors-driven failures from other mutation_failures without grepping raw logs.
evidence: 'Optional: a per-pass telemetry summary line (silent-no-op count + comment-error count) — always-on, non-fatal (pure visibility)'
The telemetry is marked optional despite being the only mechanism that surfaces which failure category triggered the non-zero exit.
findings.finding: The plan marks the per-pass telemetry summary (silent-no-op count + comment-error count) as 'Optional', but this is the primary instrumentation for the new failure mode. If omitted, operators seeing a non-zero exit cannot distinguish a comment_errors-triggered failure from any other mutation_failure without reading raw logs.
findings.location: What section: 'Optional: a per-pass telemetry summary line ... always-on, non-fatal (pure visibility)'
scenarios: Flag is activated after cleanup; reconciler starts failing; operator sees exit 1 but cannot tell from metrics/logs whether it is comment_errors or a different mutation_failure — diagnosis requires manual log grep.
findings.suggested_fix: Change 'Optional: a per-pass telemetry summary line...' to a required AC bullet: 'A structured log line (e.g. JSON or key=value) emitting comment_error_count and silent_noop_count is written at pass completion (always-on, non-fatal), so the new failure category is independently observable.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback step to the Sequencing section: e.g., 'To roll back: remove `REBAR_RECONCILER_FAIL_SILENT_NOOP=1` from reconcile-bridge.yml (or set to `0`); the reconciler reverts to warn-only immediately.'
evidence: Sequencing: 'ACTIVATE (set `REBAR_RECONCILER_FAIL_SILENT_NOOP=1` in `.github/workflows/reconcile-bridge.yml` env) only AFTER the cleanup' — acknowledges the risk but states no back-out procedure.
AC 5: '[operator-attested] Flag activated in reconcile-bridge.yml AFTER the Jira cleanup lands' — no rollback step specified.
Absence: no instruction for what to do if the flag causes unexpected failures post-activation (e.g., previously unknown capped tickets still bouncing).
findings.finding: The plan does not provide an explicit rollback/back-out step for after the flag is activated in the workflow. The T4 checklist requires a reversibility mechanism beyond mere acknowledgement of sequencing risk.
scenarios: After cleanup and flag activation, a newly capped ticket causes reconciler to fail every pass. Operator has no documented back-out — must rediscover that unsetting the var is the fix.
findings.suggested_fix: Append to the Sequencing section: 'Rollback: revert the env var to `0` (or remove it) in reconcile-bridge.yml; the reconciler immediately returns to warn-only behavior for comment_errors, restoring exit-0 on partial comment failures.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC line (or task) to update AGENTS.md (or an equivalent inline doc block at the flag check site) explaining the failure-classification contract: when `RECONCILER_FAIL_SILENT_NOOP=1`, `comment_errors` is treated as a mutation_failure and contributes to non-zero exit.
evidence: No AC line or task item mentions updating AGENTS.md, a design doc, or an ADR.

The rule 'comment_errors → mutation_failures when flag=1' is a new failure-classification contract that future contributors need to understand when extending or debugging apply_handlers.py / reconcile.py.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new behavioral contract — that comment_errors counts as a mutation_failure when the flag is ON — but names no documentation artifact (ADR, AGENTS.md, design-doc comment) where this rule will be persisted for future maintainers.
findings.location: What section and Acceptance Criteria (no doc-update AC)
scenarios: A future engineer refactors apply_handlers.py, doesn't see the flag check, silently drops the comment_errors gate, and re-introduces the silent-failure regression — because no canonical doc describes the contract.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a prominent code comment block at the new conditional in apply_handlers.py referencing the flag semantics and the sequencing requirement (activate only after Jira cleanup), and add one sentence to AGENTS.md noting that comment_errors is gated on this flag.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback step to the plan (and/or the operator-attested AC): 'To undo activation, set `REBAR_RECONCILER_FAIL_SILENT_NOOP` back to `0` (or remove it) in `reconcile-bridge.yml` and re-run the workflow — no data cleanup required.'
evidence: Plan says 'ACTIVATE (set `REBAR_RECONCILER_FAIL_SILENT_NOOP=1` in `.github/workflows/reconcile-bridge.yml` env) only AFTER the cleanup' but provides no explicit rollback instruction.
ABSENCE: no sentence of the form 'To roll back: revert the env var to 0 (or remove it) and re-run the workflow'
findings.finding: The rollback path for activating the flag is not explicitly stated. The plan specifies how to activate (set `REBAR_RECONCILER_FAIL_SILENT_NOOP=1` in reconcile-bridge.yml) but does not explicitly document the cheap undo step — reverting that env var to "0" or removing it.
findings.location: Sequencing (CRITICAL) section + AC item 5 ([operator-attested] flag activated...)
scenarios: Operator activates the flag, reconciler starts failing on capped tickets that weren't cleaned up yet, and the team needs a fast undo path without having to re-read the full plan to infer it.
findings.suggested_fix: Append to the Sequencing section: 'ROLLBACK: If activation causes unexpected failures, revert by setting `REBAR_RECONCILER_FAIL_SILENT_NOOP=0` (or removing the env entry) in `reconcile-bridge.yml`. No data changes; the next reconcile pass will exit 0 again.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a one-sentence positive rationale in the 'What' section explaining why `RECONCILER_FAIL_SILENT_NOOP` is the right flag to gate `comment_errors` promotion (e.g. same root cause, same activation timing, simpler operator surface) rather than a dedicated `RECONCILER_FAIL_COMMENT_ERROR` flag.
evidence: Plan text: 'when comment_errors is present AND _rebar_env("RECONCILER_FAIL_SILENT_NOOP")=="1"' — same flag as the silent-noop canary
No statement of WHY the single shared flag is preferred over a dedicated flag for comment_errors
The sequencing section implicitly assumes both modes are activated together ('ACTIVATE ... AFTER the cleanup'), but this is a sequencing constraint, not a rationale for flag coupling
findings.finding: The plan reuses `RECONCILER_FAIL_SILENT_NOOP` to gate two semantically distinct failure modes (silent sub-op no-ops vs. partial comment drops), but provides no positive rationale for why coupling them under one flag is the right design rather than using a dedicated flag (e.g. `RECONCILER_FAIL_COMMENT_ERROR`). Operators may want to independently promote one mode without the other — for example, activating comment-error hard-fail before resolving silent no-ops, or vice versa.
scenarios: After cleanup, operators want to activate comment-error hard-fail but NOT silent-noop hard-fail (or vice versa) — single flag makes this impossible without code changes

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
